video_title: The State Has Become a Monster | Julian Jessop
gist: Classical-liberal economist Julian Jessop argues the UK's post-2008 stagnation is self-inflicted policy failure from an oversized state (40% of GDP, near-record tax burden, 90+ taxes), and lays out an investor-relevant map of UK fiscal/political risk: Reeves's tax-led fiscal headroom, bond-market sensitivity to a Starmer/Reeves exit, supply-side energy and planning failures, and a base case that the UK can outperform Europe on AI and light-touch regulation IF it avoids an EU regulatory 'reset.'

insights.claim: Julian Jessop argues UK productivity has been structurally weak since the 2008 global financial crisis and that this — not inequality or the fading IT revolution — is the core driver of stagnant living standards, framing the slowdown as a fixable policy failure rather than an inevitable structural decline.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Bull case for UK assets is contingent on a supply-side policy reversal; without it, expect continued low-growth, low-productivity drift that caps UK equity and currency upside.
insights.quote: since then, pretty much everything has has gone horribly wrong

insights.claim: Jessop says the UK state has grown to roughly 40% of national income and argues the appropriate size is closer to 20% — implying the entire current fiscal and regulatory footprint is roughly double what he considers efficient.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Frames the magnitude of any credible 'small-state' reform program; investors should treat a halving of state share as the radical-bull scenario, not the base case.
insights.quote: I state it's about 40% of of of national income taxing or or spending. Um, I think it could be maybe half that, maybe 20%

insights.claim: Jessop identifies UK energy and housing supply as the root macro problem — the UK imports oil and gas (including North Sea gas drilled by Norway on the opposite side) and under-builds housing and infrastructure due to restrictive planning, rather than addressing demand-side symptoms.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Domestic energy production and planning liberalisation are the key catalysts to watch for UK homebuilders, energy producers and infrastructure plays; continued under-supply keeps cost-of-living and inflation pressure structurally elevated.
insights.quote: we're importing um oil and gas including you know oil and gas that Norway has dug out from the opposite side of the North Sea from us which makes no sense at all

insights.claim: Jessop contends the UK has at least 90 separate taxes — including at least one levied on something that no longer exists — creating a large 'dead weight' that disproportionately burdens small businesses while entrenching large incumbents who can afford compliance teams and lobbyists.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Regulatory and tax complexity is a structural moat for large-cap UK incumbents versus SMEs; favours scaled players and is a headwind for UK small-cap/AIM exposure.
insights.quote: we have at least you 90 separate taxes now in the UK

insights.claim: Jessop frames a 'ratchet' mechanism in UK fiscal policy whereby spending commitments like the winter fuel payment and the state-pension triple lock are nearly impossible to remove once introduced, locking in structurally rising entitlement spending.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Entitlement ratchet implies the UK fiscal trajectory bends toward higher spending and taxes regardless of governing party — a structural negative for gilts and the long-run tax burden.
insights.quote: anytime something is introduced it's then almost impossible to to take it away

insights.claim: Jessop attributes the recent spike in UK youth unemployment — rising at a pace last seen during COVID and the 2008 crisis — to government interventions, specifically the national minimum wage and day-one employment rights that make employers reluctant to hire inexperienced young workers.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Rising youth unemployment is a leading indicator of UK labour-market weakness; watch for further hiring deterioration in low-margin, labour-intensive sectors (hospitality, retail).
insights.quote: unemployment amongst young people is has spiked again. Uh the last time it rose this quickly was was during COVID and before then it was during the global financial crisis

insights.claim: Jessop describes the proposed £15/hour minimum wage and France's relatively generous minimum wage (with even higher youth unemployment than the UK) as cases where wage floors raise unemployment and hurt the intended beneficiaries.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: A £15 minimum wage would pressure margins in UK hospitality/retail toward unviability per McCormack's own coffee-shop example; a watch-item for UK consumer-facing labour-intensive businesses.
insights.quote: every historical example has shown that if you if you cap rents or massively increase minimum wages, it ends up hurting the very people you're supposed to help

insights.claim: Peter McCormack states that a £15 minimum wage would push his coffee-shop business from viable to unviable, forcing layoffs — a first-hand operator data point on how wage-floor increases convert thin-margin UK hospitality businesses into loss-makers.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host, business owner)
insights.so_what: Ground-truth on UK SME hospitality fragility under rising labour costs; reinforces the structural margin squeeze thesis for the sector.
insights.quote: it would take the business from viable to unviable

insights.claim: Jessop says Labour's manifesto pledge not to raise income tax, VAT or corporation tax forced it to raise employer National Insurance contributions instead — a 'tax on jobs' (Reeves's own prior phrase) that is now contributing to labour-market weakness.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Employer NIC increases are a direct margin and hiring headwind for UK labour-intensive employers; the manifesto tax-lock means further revenue will likely come through stealth/marginal taxes, raising policy unpredictability.
insights.quote: As Rachel Ree herself said when she was shadow chancellor, it's a tax on jobs

themes.theme: Price controls & the war on markets

insights.claim: Jessop warns of an escalating UK 'war on prices' — rent caps returning to favour, minimum-wage hikes, voluntary supermarket price caps, and courts deciding 'fair' comparative pay between shop and warehouse workers — arguing administered prices distort the signalling function markets rely on.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Spreading price administration raises regulatory risk for UK landlords, supermarkets and retail employers; rent caps specifically would reduce housing supply and quality, a negative for residential rental and homebuilder economics.
insights.quote: there seems to be this war on prices

insights.claim: Jessop argues Reform UK's proposal to exempt overtime above 40 hours/week from income tax — modelled on a US idea — would fail as it did in France, where well-off people with accountants reclassified hours as overtime, lower-paid workers gained nothing, and tax revenue fell.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Signals that headline 'pro-grafter' tax giveaways are gameable and revenue-negative; investors should discount populist tax-cut pledges from both Reform and Greens as fiscally unfunded.
insights.quote: all that happened is that relatively well-off people with clever accountants fiddled their arrangements to make sure that more of their hours counted as overtime

insights.claim: Jessop frames regulatory complexity with the maxim that 'the bigger the surface area of the state, the more chance there is to exploit it,' arguing dense rules entrench large businesses (who can staff HR, legal and compliance) at the expense of small competitors.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Reinforces that rising UK regulation is a competitive moat for incumbents — structurally favours large-cap defensives over UK small-caps and new entrants.
insights.quote: the bigger the surface area of the state, the more chance there is to exploit it

insights.claim: Jessop says UK financial markets have reacted negatively every time Keir Starmer or Rachel Reeves looked at risk of leaving, because the market views the alternatives (e.g. Andy Burnham) as worse for fiscal stability — making Reeves's perceived commitment to fiscal rules a stabiliser for gilts and sterling.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: A leadership change away from Reeves is a tail risk for gilts and sterling; the bond market is effectively pricing Reeves as the fiscal-credibility anchor — watch Labour leadership signals as a gilt catalyst.
insights.quote: every time there have been concerns that either Starmmer or Rachel Reeves might might go, the markets have reacted negatively

insights.claim: Jessop notes Reeves built extra fiscal headroom into the budget but did so by raising taxes rather than controlling spending, which markets disliked even as they credit her commitment to the fiscal rules.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Tax-led rather than spending-led consolidation keeps the UK tax burden rising and growth suppressed; the quality of any future headroom-building (taxes vs cuts) is a gilt and growth signal.
insights.quote: she's done it by raising taxes, not by controlling spending

insights.claim: Jessop argues the 2022 Truss/Kwarteng 'mini budget' bond-market blowup was already calming and was driven partly by unlucky timing — the Bank of England starting to sell gilts (QT) into a fragile market amid globally rising rates — rather than purely by the policy itself.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Reframes UK gilt fragility as partly a QT/liquidity timing problem; relevant for assessing how future unfunded fiscal moves could interact with BoE balance-sheet operations to trigger disorderly gilt selloffs.
insights.quote: the bank of England was starting to sell government bonds back onto the market so the bond market was fragile interest rates were rising around the rest of the world

insights.claim: Jessop says the Greens and Reform UK are between them polling over 50% by promising popular but unfunded policies, with Reform paradoxically favouring nationalisation and keeping the pension triple lock — i.e. expanding rather than shrinking the state.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Rising vote share for fiscally expansionary insurgent parties raises medium-term UK policy and gilt risk; nationalisation rhetoric is a specific overhang for UK utilities and rail/water-adjacent equities.
insights.quote: between them, they're probably polling more than 50% in the poll

insights.claim: Jessop names Gary Stevenson ('Gary's Economics') as a credible-seeming public advocate lending intellectual cover to redistributionist/rent-control policies (alongside Zack Polanski/Greens), illustrating how city-trader backstories are used to legitimise interventionist economics.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Maps the influencers shaping UK left-wing economic narrative; their rising platform is a leading indicator of redistributionist policy momentum and associated UK asset-tax/rent-control risk.
insights.quote: Gary Stevenson is one um v various others I can mention as well who who who have a sort of veneer of intellectual respons respectability

themes.theme: NHS, public-sector productivity & state IT failure

insights.claim: Jessop argues the NHS delivers one of the worst standards of care in the developed world and identifies three root causes — unreliable Treasury-dependent funding, lack of competition stifling innovation, and heavy unionisation resisting change — while pointing to mixed public-private models in Europe and Asia (not the US) as the reform template.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: A shifting NHS reform debate toward European-style mixed models is a long-horizon tailwind for UK private healthcare providers and insurers; watch polling shifts as the catalyst.
insights.quote: the hugely bloated National Health Service um which provides one of the worst standards of care anywhere in the in the developed world

insights.claim: Both speakers point to a thriving private GP market (e.g. a £50 walk-in 'medic centre' in Bedford) as a free-market response to NHS waiting lists, suggesting growing consumer willingness to pay out-of-pocket for faster UK primary care.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: Out-of-pocket private primary-care demand is rising structurally — a growth signal for UK private clinics, diagnostics and health-services businesses.
insights.quote: £50 for an appointment

insights.claim: Jessop argues the UK government is systematically bad at large IT and procurement — citing the Post Office Horizon scandal and warning a state digital ID scheme will be late, broken at launch and leak data — because a monopoly buyer over-pays for a single system with no competitive selection.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Flags execution risk in UK state digital programmes; relevant for assessing government-IT contractors and the political durability of any digital-ID rollout.
insights.quote: the the NHS of course would only want to have one system as well for which it would massively overpay and we saw the problems with that with the the post office scandal

insights.claim: Jessop credits the 2010-era Cameron government with materially improving public-sector productivity (without large real spending cuts — spending rose but was controlled), and says public-sector productivity is now going backwards because the current government is throwing money at services without structural reform.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Declining public-sector productivity under unreformed higher spending implies poor fiscal multipliers and worsening value-for-money — a structural drag on UK growth and the public finances.
insights.quote: it's now going backwards because all the government is doing is throwing more money at it rather than structurally reforming it

themes.theme: Comparative models: Milei, China, Singapore, Poland

insights.claim: Jessop cites Javier Milei's Argentina as a positive real-world test of shock free-market reform — where ~100+ economists predicted surging poverty and collapse but roughly the opposite occurred — and invokes the Laffer curve and anti-nationalisation arguments Milei popularised.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Milei's apparent success is the template free-market reformers will cite for the UK; useful as a base-rate reference for how crisis-then-reform can re-rate a distressed economy and its assets.
insights.quote: pretty much the opposite has has happened

insights.claim: Jessop argues China's and Singapore's edge is institutional — the absence of frequent elections lets China plan long-term and build fast (contrasting with HS2 delays), while Singapore recruits genuinely impressive officials from military/business/academia, implying a big state only works with high-calibre administrators.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Frames why UK state capacity is weak (short electoral cycles, low-calibre administrators); relevant to judging whether large UK infrastructure programmes can ever deliver on time/budget.
insights.quote: I honestly think we will colonize Mars more quickly than we will complete HS2

insights.claim: Jessop says Poland is on track to overtake the UK on living standards within 5-10 years, crediting its simple tax system, straightforward planning rules and well-run economy — a concrete benchmark of relative UK decline.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Relative-decline benchmark: Poland overtaking the UK is a tangible marker of Central European convergence — supports a structural preference for reform-track CEE exposure over stagnant Western Europe.
insights.quote: a lot of people are horrified by the prospect that Poland will overtake the UK at some point in the next 5 to 10 years on living standards

insights.claim: Jessop's constructive base case is that the UK is well-placed to benefit from the AI revolution — better than the rest of Europe — and could outperform Europe by moving toward a US-style light-touch, business-friendly model rather than re-aligning with the EU.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: The UK bull case is explicitly conditioned on AI adoption plus regulatory divergence from the EU; investors should treat 'UK as European AI/light-touch outperformer' as the upside scenario to monitor.
insights.quote: We're well well placed to benefit from the AI revolution. Um certainly compared to the rest of Europe

insights.claim: Jessop, a self-described Brexit optimist, flags the EU-UK 'reset' as his key downside risk — warning that being dragged back toward the overregulated EU model would undermine the UK's potential to be a light-touch, high-business-culture outperformer.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: The EU-UK regulatory 'reset' is the specific policy swing factor between the UK's bull and bear paths; its direction is a watch-item for the UK growth/regulation re-rating thesis.
insights.quote: I'm very concerned about the EU UK reset and the direction of travel there

insights.claim: Jessop argues equity and financial markets take genuinely long-term views — evidenced by highly valued companies that are years from profitability — directly contradicting the common claim that 'the City' is short-termist, and contrasting this with politicians' 5-year (or 24-hour news) horizons.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: Defends long-duration/growth equity valuation logic against the short-termism critique; supports patience with pre-profit growth names where markets price long-run potential.
insights.quote: there are lots of companies that haven't yet made profits and probably won't make profits for a very long time then nonetheless very highly valued by the markets because they can see the the potential

insights.claim: Jessop compares the UK to Japan — a wealthy country with near-zero growth for decades that tolerates stagnation because it started rich — invoking the 'boiling frog' analogy and suggesting a genuine crisis might be necessary to force reform.
insights.speaker: Julian Jessop (economist, guest)
insights.so_what: The Japan parallel implies the UK can drift in low-growth stagnation for a long time without forcing change — a base case of muddle-through rather than either crisis or reform, capping UK asset re-rating potential.
insights.quote: It's had next to no growth for decades now. But because it starts from a reasonably comfortable position, people can sort of put up with things
